WI.hero_title: Wisconsin: Where ThedaCare Leads and Froedtert Lags
WI.hero_subtitle: The state with the highest median transparency — and the sharpest system-level contrasts.
WI.intro_text: ThedaCare achieves 100% transparency at all 9 facilities. Froedtert Hospital — Milwaukee's flagship — discloses rates for only 6 payers. UW Health discloses 112 payer contracts but remains on the outdated v2.0.0 schema.
findings: ThedaCare is the transparency gold standard: 100% payer rate disclosure at all 9 facilities, up to 59 payers, current v3.0.0 schema, updated March 2026.
Froedtert Hospital — Milwaukee's largest, Level I Trauma Center — discloses rates for only 6 payers. A 25-bed critical access hospital in rural Boscobel (Gundersen) discloses more.

UW Health discloses the most payer contracts in the state (112) using percentage-of-gross methodology, but its file is stale (January 2025) and still on v2.0.0 — at risk for CMS enforcement beginning April 2026.
Ascension's three Wisconsin facilities are the least transparent (27–38%), consistent with national reporting on Ascension's disclosure approach.
Marshfield's 5% cash discount is effectively no discount — a patient saves $50 on a $1,000 procedure. The lowest meaningful discount in the state.
Gundersen's intra-system cash discount variance spans 21% to 62% across seven facilities — evidence of facility-level pricing autonomy.

MN.hero_subtitle: A consumer-governed nonprofit that hides what insurance actually pays.
MN.intro_text: HealthPartners — both an insurer and a hospital system — scores 6.9% at Regions Hospital. They give patients generous cash discounts (53–75% off gross) while hiding what insured patients' carriers actually negotiate.
findings: HealthPartners Regions Hospital — Level I Trauma, 454 beds, consumer-governed nonprofit — discloses payer rates on 6.9% of rows. The lowest non-zero score in the three-state dataset.
All four HealthPartners facilities score below 12% — the only system in either state entirely below 20%. Yet their cash discounts are the highest in Minnesota (53–75%).
Mayo Clinic Rochester discloses rates for 1,102 payer/plan combinations — 10x more than any other hospital in any state. The richest single chargemaster file in the dataset.
Allina Health achieves 99.8–100% across all 5 analyzed facilities — proving full disclosure is operationally feasible for a large multi-hospital system.
Essentia Brainerd (St. Joseph's Medical Center) scores 99.6% with 39 payers — one of the most transparent hospitals in the dataset and the strongest local data anchor.
M Health Fairview shows good percentage transparency (70–90%) but only 2–4 payers per facility — the structure has rates but for very few contracts.

IL.hero_subtitle: Northwestern and Rush comply fully. UChicago publishes almost nothing.
IL.intro_text: Three of Chicago's four academic flagships comply comprehensively. UChicago Medical Center discloses payer rates for only 2.9% of items — exclusively DRG bundles — while individual line items show only list prices.
findings: UChicago Medical Center discloses payer rates for 2.9% of items (2,302 of 78,504). Payer data exists only on DRG bundles; 97% of items show only gross charges and cash prices. Strategic selective disclosure.
Northwestern Memorial Hospital achieves ~95% transparency with 32 payers across v3.0.0 JSON — comprehensive disclosure from Chicago's largest hospital.
Rush University Medical Center hits 100% with 35 payers — proving Chicago's academic centers can comply fully.
Advocate Health is remarkably uniform: all 11 facilities cluster at 60–70% with exactly 18 payers and a 50% cash discount. Centralized but incomplete.
HSHS's Illinois hospitals (100%, 30–34 payers) outperform Ascension's Illinois presence (32–67%) — smaller system, better disclosure.
Loyola Gottlieb offers an 81% cash discount — the highest in the three-state dataset — while disclosing only 56% of payer rates.
